MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines (Norma, an averaged perceptron, and the deep bi-LSTM of Bollmann & Søgaard 2016, including a multi-task variant) using the same Anselm corpus and word accuracy metrics. Comparisons appear fair and thorough (held-out manuscript B for development, same evaluation metrics), and the authors provide detailed analyses of differences (weight comparisons, error-overlap, learned projections, saliency) and explicitly acknowledge when combinations (MTL+attention) hurt performance. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and key competing approaches are cited and discussed. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Norma, the averaged perceptron approach of Wieling et al. 2009, and Bollmann & Søgaard 2016's deep bi-LSTM) using the same Anselm corpus and word-level accuracy/error metrics. Comparisons appear fair (same data splits with a held-out development manuscript, reported hyperparameters) and the authors report when multi-task or combined setups perform worse (e.g., MTL+attention decline). The work also includes detailed analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, vector projections, saliency) and states that related work coverage is adequate, supporting a comprehensive and fair evaluation.

Comparisons appear fair and thorough (held-out manuscript B for development, same evaluation metrics), and the authors provide detailed analyses of differences (weight comparisons, error-overlap, learned projections, saliency) and explicitly acknowledge when combinations (MTL+attention) hurt performance. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and key competing approaches are cited and discussed.

The work also includes detailed analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, vector projections, saliency) and states that related work coverage is adequate, supporting a comprehensive and fair evaluation.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is squarely within NLP scope (historical text normalization) and uses methods familiar to top-tier NLP venues (character-level encoder–decoder LSTMs, attention, beam search, multi-task learning) with appropriate baselines (Norma, perceptron, bi-LSTM), datasets (Anselm corpus, CELEX), and standard evaluation (word accuracy, error analysis) evaluated across many manuscripts. Its empirical and analytical treatment (44 texts, multifaceted analyses) suits the audience and standards of leading NLP conferences. Minor scope concerns arise for a general ML venue—its contribution is primarily an applied/empirical advance within NLP rather than a broadly novel ML method—but it is a good fit for top NLP venues. | B(4): This work is squarely within NLP topics (historical text normalization) and uses methods of broad interest to the community (character-level encoder–decoder models, attention, beam search, and multi-task learning with a grapheme-to-phoneme auxiliary task) evaluated on a large set of manuscripts (Anselm corpus, 44 texts) against reasonable baselines. Evaluation practices (word accuracy, error analysis, comparisons to Norma and prior neural and non-neural approaches) and the multifaceted analysis match expectations for a top NLP venue. Minor scope concern: the focus on Early New High German historical manuscripts makes the application somewhat specialized and may be less central to general ML audiences, so it is a better fit for top NLP than for a general ML conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies a multi-task learning setup using a grapheme-to-phoneme auxiliary task to encoder–decoder normalization and reports a modest ~2% absolute improvement over prior state-of-the-art, which is a useful empirical contribution. However, the approach largely combines existing techniques (character-level seq2seq with attention, beam search, lexical filtering, and standard MTL with CELEX-derived G2P data) rather than introducing a fundamentally new method. The comprehensive analyses across 44 Anselm texts and the observation that MTL can induce attention-like focus are informative to experts, but overall the contribution is best characterized as an incremental advance rather than a major novelty. | B(3): The paper applies established techniques (character-level encoder–decoder with attention and multi-task learning) to historical text normalization, but its main novelty is the specific auxiliary task choice (grapheme-to-phoneme training from CELEX) and the empirical finding that this MTL setup yields a ≈2% absolute improvement and particular attention behaviors. The problem itself is well-studied and related baselines (e.g., Bollmann & Søgaard 2016, Norma) are compared, so the contribution is an incremental, non-trivial adaptation and analysis rather than a fundamentally new method. The extensive multifaceted analysis across 44 manuscripts strengthens the empirical novelty but does not constitute a wholly new paradigm, so the work would inform experts without surprising them greatly.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a multi-task learning setup using a grapheme-to-phoneme auxiliary task to encoder–decoder normalization and reports a modest ~2% absolute improvement over prior state-of-the-art, which is a useful empirical contribution. The extensive multifaceted analysis across 44 manuscripts strengthens the empirical novelty but does not constitute a wholly new paradigm, so the work would inform experts without surprising them greatly.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are broad and comparative: evaluation across 44 Anselm texts, comparisons to multiple strong baselines (Norma, averaged perceptron, deep bi-LSTM), and investigation of architectural variants (attention, beam search, lexical filtering, MTL). The paper includes several ablation-style analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, learned projections, saliency) and reports negative results (MTL+attention declines) and clear limitations (scarce per-manuscript data, token-in-isolation problem, hyperparameters tuned on held-out manuscript B). Related work coverage is described as adequate and hyperparameters are reported, but code is not mentioned and some reproducibility choices (single-manuscript hyperparameter tuning) and lack of released code are minor gaps preventing a full 5. | B(4): Experiments are broadly comprehensive: evaluations span 44 Anselm texts, compare to multiple strong baselines, and include many analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, vector projections, saliency) and architectural variants (attention, beam search, filtering, MTL). Ablation-style comparisons and a reported negative result (MTL combined with attention hurts accuracy) show sensitivity to modeling choices. Related work appears adequately covered and limitations are discussed (scarce per-manuscript training data, models work on isolated words, hyperparameters tuned on a held-out manuscript). Reproducibility is helped by reported hyperparameters but is weakened by no mention of released code and the reliance on a single development manuscript, so a minor gap remains.

Reproducibility is helped by reported hyperparameters but is weakened by no mention of released code and the reliance on a single development manuscript, so a minor gap remains.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation through methods and experiments, with the main contributions (MTL with G2P auxiliary task, attention analyses, evaluation over 44 Anselm texts) stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and sufficient (hyperparameters reported; κ=0.834 reported for annotation agreement), and limitations (data scarcity, context-free normalization, MTL+attention drop) are explicitly discussed. Figures/tables and multiple analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, vector projections, saliency) are comprehensive and informative, and overall writing quality is described as good, so only minor presentation polish would be needed. | B(4): The paper is well organized (clear progression from architecture and MTL method to experiments on the Anselm corpus and CELEX auxiliary data) and states its main contributions early (improvement via G2P MTL, attention behavior analysis). Notation and methodological details are consistently reported (character-level encoder–decoder variants, beam size, lexical filter, hyperparameters) and the multifaceted analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, vector projections, saliency) imply effective figures/tables and examples. Writing is generally clear and grammatically sound, with minor presentation issues such as tuning on a single held-out manuscript and no explicit mention of released code that could have been stated more explicitly for reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation through methods and experiments, with the main contributions (MTL with G2P auxiliary task, attention analyses, evaluation over 44 Anselm texts) stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and sufficient (hyperparameters reported; κ=0.834 reported for annotation agreement), and limitations (data scarcity, context-free normalization, MTL+attention drop) are explicitly discussed. Figures/tables and multiple analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, vector projections, saliency) are comprehensive and informative, and overall writing quality is described as good, so only minor presentation polish would be needed.

Writing is generally clear and grammatically sound, with minor presentation issues such as tuning on a single held-out manuscript and no explicit mention of released code that could have been stated more explicitly for reproducibility.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem (historical text normalization) and reports a meaningful empirical improvement (~2% absolute over prior SOTA) across a large set of manuscripts (44 texts from the Anselm corpus), indicating robustness. Its demonstration that multi-task learning with a grapheme-to-phoneme auxiliary task can improve normalization and shape attention behaviour provides a concrete methodological insight that is likely to change how researchers design low-resource seq2seq normalization systems. The multifaceted analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, saliency) and use of CELEX make this work a useful foothold for follow-up studies, though limitations (scarce per-manuscript data, models operating on isolated wordforms, and mixed interactions of MTL+attention) temper its immediate transformative reach. | B(3): The paper tackles a relevant problem in historical text normalization and reports a meaningful empirical gain (~2% absolute over prior SOTA) using character-level encoder–decoder models with a grapheme-to-phoneme multi-task auxiliary task. Its extensive evaluation across 44 Anselm manuscripts and detailed analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, saliency) provide useful evidence and insights—notably that MTL can induce attention-like focusing—which can influence follow-up work. However, limitations (scarce per-manuscript training data, models operating on isolated wordforms, and a drop in accuracy when combining MTL with explicit attention) and the niche application area temper its broader or transformative impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem (historical text normalization) and reports a meaningful empirical improvement (~2% absolute over prior SOTA) across a large set of manuscripts (44 texts from the Anselm corpus), indicating robustness. Its demonstration that multi-task learning with a grapheme-to-phoneme auxiliary task can improve normalization and shape attention behaviour provides a concrete methodological insight that is likely to change how researchers design low-resource seq2seq normalization systems. The multifaceted analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, saliency) and use of CELEX make this work a useful foothold for follow-up studies, though limitations (scarce per-manuscript data, models operating on isolated wordforms, and mixed interactions of MTL+attention) temper its immediate transformative reach.

Its extensive evaluation across 44 Anselm manuscripts and detailed analyses (weight comparisons, error-overlap, saliency) provide useful evidence and insights—notably that MTL can induce attention-like focusing—which can influence follow-up work. However, limitations (scarce per-manuscript training data, models operating on isolated wordforms, and a drop in accuracy when combining MTL with explicit attention) and the niche application area temper its broader or transformative impact.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the main claims are supported by experiments across a large set of manuscripts (43 Anselm texts) and comparisons to appropriate baselines (Norma, averaged perceptron, Bollmann & Søgaard), and the paper reports relevant metrics (word accuracy, error counts, Fleiss’ kappa). No mathematical proofs are required and none are claimed. Methodologically there are minor concerns: hyperparameters were tuned on a single held-out manuscript (B) and no statistical significance testing is reported, and the paper itself reports that combining MTL with attention decreased accuracy, which complicates the claim that MTL reliably learns attention-like behavior. These are minor issues that do not invalidate the main experimental findings. | B(4): The core claims are supported by comprehensive experiments over the Anselm corpus (44 texts, 43 used for evaluation with manuscript B held out) showing an absolute ~2% gain and comparisons against sensible baselines (Norma, averaged perceptron, and the deep bi-LSTM tagger). There are no mathematical proofs to check and none appear incorrect. The experimental methodology is largely sound—character-level encoder–decoder models with attention, beam search, lexical filtering, and a grapheme-to-phoneme MTL auxiliary task, evaluated with word accuracy and error counts—however the paper does not report statistical significance testing and hyperparameters were tuned on a single held-out manuscript, which are minor weaknesses. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, though reproducibility details (code) and significance analyses would strengthen the claims.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the main claims are supported by experiments across a large set of manuscripts (43 Anselm texts) and comparisons to appropriate baselines (Norma, averaged perceptron, Bollmann & Søgaard), and the paper reports relevant metrics (word accuracy, error counts, Fleiss’ kappa). No mathematical proofs are required and none are claimed. Methodologically there are minor concerns: hyperparameters were tuned on a single held-out manuscript (B) and no statistical significance testing is reported, and the paper itself reports that combining MTL with attention decreased accuracy, which complicates the claim that MTL reliably learns attention-like behavior. These are minor issues that do not invalidate the main experimental findings.

reviewer_b.justification: The core claims are supported by comprehensive experiments over the Anselm corpus (44 texts, 43 used for evaluation with manuscript B held out) showing an absolute ~2% gain and comparisons against sensible baselines (Norma, averaged perceptron, and the deep bi-LSTM tagger). There are no mathematical proofs to check and none appear incorrect. The experimental methodology is largely sound—character-level encoder–decoder models with attention, beam search, lexical filtering, and a grapheme-to-phoneme MTL auxiliary task, evaluated with word accuracy and error counts—however the paper does not report statistical significance testing and hyperparameters were tuned on a single held-out manuscript, which are minor weaknesses. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, though reproducibility details (code) and significance analyses would strengthen the claims.

main_claims: Using multi-task learning with a grapheme-to-phoneme auxiliary task improves historical text normalization performance, yielding an absolute ~2% increase over prior state-of-the-art.
Multi-task learning can learn to focus attention during decoding in ways remarkably similar to explicit attention mechanisms.

Encoder–decoder architectures (with attention, beam search, filtering, and/or MTL) outperform baselines such as Norma, an averaged perceptron, and the deep bi-LSTM sequence-labeling approach of Bollmann & Søgaard (2016) on Early New High German texts.

The paper provides a multifaceted analysis across many manuscripts (44 texts from the Anselm corpus), including weight comparisons, error-overlap analysis, learned vector projections, and saliency analyses to study effects of MTL and attention.
_summary.methodology: Character-level encoder–decoder LSTM architectures are trained to map historical wordforms to modern normalizations, with variants including soft attention, beam-search decoding (beam size 5), a lexical filter using CELEX+training targets, and a multi-task learning setup that alternates training with a grapheme-to-phoneme auxiliary task derived from CELEX.
datasets: Anselm corpus (Early New High German; 44 texts, 43 used for evaluation after leaving out manuscript B for development)
CELEX German lexical database (phonetic transcriptions; 365,530 wordforms)
baselines: Averaged perceptron with Levenshtein-based alignment (Wieling et al., 2009)
Deep bi-LSTM sequential tagger (Bollmann & Søgaard, 2016) evaluated in standard and multi-task setups
Norma tool (rule-based and Levenshtein-based algorithms; Bollmann, 2012)
metrics: Fleiss' kappa (reported κ=0.834 for word-level agreement)
limitations: Training data is scarce: individual texts are short (≈4,200–13,200 tokens) and hyperparameters were set on a single manuscript (B) held out of final averages.
Models consider word forms in isolation (no token context), which is problematic for ambiguous cases (e.g., 'jn' → 'in' or 'ihn').
Combining multi-task learning with attention led to a decline in word accuracy in their experiments.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - historical text normalization (character-level neural sequence-to-sequence models)
